content.full_text:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the revolving door
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 This letter should not be construed
as assistance on any conduct that may have already taken place; the Commission does not
provide advice with respect to past conduct. (See Regulation 18329(bX8XA).) In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices
Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders
assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your question is general in
nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.2
Fern PolrrrcAt- PRACTICEs CouuISSIoN
.128 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-23?9
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
April9, 2009
Steve Mihara
26 Snyder Way
Fremont, Califomia 94536
RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-09-047
Dear Mr. Mihara:
Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We
therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other laws such as the post-
government employment restrictions of Public Contract Code Section 10411. You may
wish to consult the Attomey General's office regarding these provisions.
QUESTTONS
l. What restrictions do the Act's revolving door prohibitions place on your
activities on behalf of a local government agency regarding contacts with your former
employer, a state government agency?
' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 I 000 through 9 I 014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
PoliticalPractices Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory ret'erences are to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
: lntbrmal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
tbrmal written advice. (Section 83 I l4; Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)


File No. I-09-047
Page No. 2
2. May you perform auditing work as a self-employed contractor for health care
plans and submit those audits to MRMIB under the MRMIB follow up program.
CONCLUSIONS
l. You are subject to the provisions of the Act's "one-year ban" and "permanent"
ban as described below. You may not communicate with yourformer employer, for
compensation, on behalf of another for a period o, on.-y"ur after you leave siate service
if that contact is made for the pu{pose of influencing administrative or legislative actions
or any discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, oi."rocation of a
permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods o, prop"rty.
Additionally, you are prohibited from "switching sides', and participaiing-, foi
compensation, in any specific proceeding involving the State of Caiifornia or assisting
others in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which you participated while
employed by the state.
2. Because MRMIB is not your former employer, the Act's "one-year ban" does
not apply to any contacts you make with this agency, so long as its budgei p"rrorrr"l, and
other operations are not subject to the control of the Department of tvlaiaged Heath Care
(DMHC). However, the Act's permanent ban would privent you from "ririt"hing sides"
and representing your current employer with respect tb any foilow up audits because you
participated in audits of that health care provider while you were engaged in state service.
FACTS
You were employed by the DMHC, Financial Oversight Division from May 2005
through January 2009. At the time of your departure, you held a position as an Examiner
IV (supervisor)' For the last two years at DMHC, you were primaiily responsible for
overseeing an audit program to review the medical loss ratios for thi fteatttry Families
program administered by the Managed Risk Medical Insurance Board (the..!VIRMIB").
The Healthy Families program audits were the result of an interagency contract between
the DMHC and the MRMIB. Audit guidelines were established by pir4ffC so rhar there
would be no cross communication between the MRMIB audits ana th" DMHC audits and
thus insulate the operations as much as possible (at the urging of health care plans to
avoid double jeopardy).
Santa Clara County Health Authority ("SCCHA") is a licensed health care plan
under the Knox-Keene Act. The DMHC exercises oversight of health care plans under
this act. During the last two years at the DMHC, you did not participate in any financial
monitoring activities, routine examinations, or non-rout-ine examinations of thl SCCHA.
SCCHA has a contract with the MRMIB to provide services to low income
children under the Healthy Families Program. During the last two-and-a-half years, you
performed or supported the audits of approximately fb MRMB audits, one of which was


File No. l-09-047
Page No. 3
on the SCCHA. MRMIB has not completed its review of the audit results nor has it, as
yet, recommended the implementation of any corrective action plans for the audit you
conducted of SCCHA.
You left the employment of the DMHC for a position at SCCHA when you were
offered your culrent position several months after the conclusion of the MRMIB audit of
SCCHA. MRMIB has been planning to incorporate a follow-up audit procedure in future
contracts with health care plans, which may require a health plan to incur a second audit
to be completed at its expense in the event that the plan does not perform satisfactorily
against their contracted Medical Loss Ratio rate. [n anticipation of leaving the DMHC,
you offered to the MRMIB that you would be available to conduct those eiams (the
DMHC may not be able to conduct the exams based on budget and staffing limitations).
Under such a scenario, the health care plans will be contracting directly *ith you (if you
are an acceptable audit source to MRMIB), giving you access to the privacy information
("HIPPA") necessary to properly conduct the examination. MRMIBwould thereby
receive a suflrmary audit report similar to what it currently receives from DMHC
(MRMIB does not have authority to receive the privacy information obtained during the
course of the examination).
The DMHC has taken the position that"zero torerance" applies in any
communications with you as a former employee. This has resulted in employees at the
DMHC not communicating with you (and presumably other DMHC employees; on..any
and all business issues."
ANALYSIS
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-
governmental employment provisions under the Act, colloquially known as the
"revolving door" prohibitions. In addition, Section 87407 prohibits certain state and local
officials from making, participating in making, or using their official position to influence
decisions affecting persons with whom they are negotiating employment, or have any
arrangement conceming employment.3 (Aiso see Regulat{on rc1a7.)
Post-Governmental Employment provisions
One-Year Ban - The "one-year ban" prohibits a former state employee from
appearing before or communicating with, for compensation, his or her former agency for
the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any discrJtionary
act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license,
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (See Section 97406;
Regulation 18746.1.)
3 Because you have already left state service, we do not address this issue. The Commission does
not provide advice with respect to past conduct.


File No. l-09-047
Page No.4
The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who
holds a position that is designated or should be designated in the agency', .onfli.t-of-
interest code. (Section 87406(dXl); Regulation 18746.1(a)(Z).)4 ihe ban applies for 12
months from the date the employee leaves state office or employment, whicl is defined
as the date the employee permanently leaves state service or takes a leave of absence.
(Regulation I 8746. l(b)(l) and (2).)
While in effect, the one-year ban applies only when a former employee or official
is being compensated for his or her appearances or communications before his or her
former agency on behalf of any person as an agent, attorney, or representative of that
person. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(3) and (a).)
ln contrast to the permanent ban, which only applies to 'Judicial or quasi-judicial"
proceedings, the one-year ban applies to "any appearance or communication madl for the
purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action or influencing any action or
proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit,
license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property.', (Regulation
18746.1(b)(5).) An appearance or communication is for the "prrpor. of infliencing" if it
is made for the "principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying,
opposing, delaying, or advancing the action or proceeding.;' lRegulatio-n rclqe.z.i ln
appearance or corlmunication includes, but is not limited to, conversing by telephone or
in person, correspondfng in writing or by electronic transmission, attenlini a meeting,
and delivering or sending any communication. (/d.)
S ection 82002(a) defines .,adminis trative action,' as :
" (a) 'Administrative action' means the proposal, drafting, development,
consideration, amendment, enactment, or defeat by any state agenty of
any rule, regulation, or other action in any ratemaking proceeding o. *y
quasi-legislative proceeding, which shall include any proceeding-governed
by Chapter 3.5 (commencing with Section 11340) oi part I of Division 3
of Title 2."
Section 82037 defines "legislative action" as:
"'Legislative action' means the drafting, introduction, consideration,
modification, enactment or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment,
report, nomination or other matter by the Legislature or by either house or
any committee, subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof, or by a
member or employee of the Legislature acting in his official capacity.
o A governmental employee should be designated in his or her agency's conflict-of-interest code
if the employee makes or participates in making governmental decisions that have a reasonably foreseeable
material effect on any financial interest. (Section 97302.)


File No. I-09-047
Page No.5
'Legislative action' also means the action of the Governor in approving or
vetoing any bill."
Finally, appearances and communications are prohibited only if they are before a
state agency that the public official worked for or represented or a state agency..which
budget, personnel, and other operations" are subjectlo the control of a sta-te agency the
public official worked for or represented. (Regulation 18746.1(bX6).) This iould apply
to any covered communications you may have with DMHC.S
However, not all communications are prohibited by the one-year ban.
Appearances or communications before a former state agency employer, made as part of
"seryices performed to administer, implement, or fulfill the requirlmlnts of an existing
permit, license, grant, contract, or sale agreement may be excluded from the [one-yearj
prohibitions . . . provided the services do not involve the issuance, amendment, awarding,
or revocation of any of these actions or proceedings." (Regulation IB746.l(b)(5XA);
Quiring Advice Letter, No. 4-03-272; Hanan Advice Lettir, No. I-00-209.)
Additionally, Regulat ion 187 46.2(bX I )-(4) provides that appearances or
communications are not restricted under the one-year ban, if an individual:
"(l) Participates as a panelist or formal speaker at a conference or similar
public event for educational puqposes or to disseminate research and the
subject matter does not pertain to a specific action or proceeding;
"(2) Attends a general informational meeting, seminar, or similar event;
"(3) Requests information concerning any matter of public record; or
"(4) Communicates with the press."
We have also advised that a former agency official may, without violating the
one-year ban, draft proposals on a client's behalf to be submitied to the agency ,o long u,
the former employee is not identified in connection with the client's efforts to influence
administrative action. (CookAdvice Letter, No. 4-95-321; Harrison Advice Letter, No.
A-92-289-) Similarly, a former agency official may use his or her expertise to advise
clients on the procedural requirements, plans, or policies of the official's former agency
so long as the employee is not identified with the employer's efforts to influence the
agency. (Perry Advice Letter, No. 4-94-004.)
From the facts provided, you are a former designated employee of the DMHC and
are now employed with a local governmental agency. As such, uppeururces before or
5 You have not provided any information about whether or not MRMIB is an agency with budget,
personnel, and other operations that are subject to the control of DMHC. For purposes Jf oui analysis, we
assume it is not.


File No. I-09-047
Page No. 6
communications with the DMHC on behalf of the local governmental agency are
prohibited for a one-year period if made for the purpose of influencing i'd*irirt utive or
legislative action or influencing any action involving the issuance, amendment, awarding,
or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or
property.
You have indicated that you have had certain problems regarding your former
employer engaging in any communications whatsoever with you.-Ho*Jrlr, as indicated
above, you are not prohibited from making any contacts whaisoever with your former
employer. You may prepare administrative an financial filings for SCCHA as required by
DMHC or MRMIB so long as these filing are not for the purpose of influencing
administrative action. Even if they are, you may still prepare the filing so long as your
name does not appear on the filings. you may se*e ai the contact perion for -
coordination of information requests between SCCHA and DMHC or MRMIB so long as
you do not engage in any activities to influence administrative or legislative action as
outlined above. You may assist your culrent employer in implemen-ting any corrective
plans that the DMHC or MRMIB may impose, so long ur yo, do not -it" urry
communications with DMHC for the purpose of influencing any administrativl action or
any discretionary act involving possible amendments to thJrequirements imposed.
Finally, because MRMIB is not your former employer, the Act-'s revolving door
provisions do not apply to any communication you may engage in with thit ug"n"y.
Hopefully, this assistance will help you in making future determinations of
whethOr any particular appearance or communication is prohibited under the one-year
ban. If you need further assistance relating to a specifi" appeurarrce or corlmunication,
you should seek further advice providing all relevant factJ.-
Permanent Ban - The "permanent ban" prohibits a former state employee from
"switching sides" and participating, for compensation, in any specific proceedirg
involving tht ltuf of California or assisting others in the proceeding ii the proceeding is
one in which the former state employee participated whiliemployeJby rhe state. (See
Sections 87 401-87 402; Regulation I 874 l. l.)
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any judicial, quasi-judicial, or
other proceeding in which you participated while ybu served ai a state administrative
official. "'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding,
application, request for a ruling or other determination, contract, tlaim, contriversy,
investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or other particular matter involving a specific
party or parties in any court or state administrative agency . . .." (Section d'z+ob1c;.y
Additionally, an official is considered to have "participatid" in a proceeding if he or she
took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantially through-decision,-approval,
disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering rdui"e on a substantiaibasis,
investigation, or use of confidential information . . .." (Section g7400(d).)


File No. l-09-047
Page No. 7
"The permanent ban does not apply to a 'new' proceeding even in cases where the
new proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in which the official had
participated. A 'new' proceeding not subject to the permanent ban typically involves
different parties, a different subject matter, or different factual issues iro* ihor"
considered in previous proceedings." (Risr Advice Letter, No. A-04-lg7; also see
Donovan Advice Letter, No. I-03-119.) New contracts with the emplbyee's former
agency in which the former employee did not participate are considered new proceedings.
(Leslie Advice Letter, No. I-89-649.) A new contract is one that is based or, ne*
consideration and new terms, even if it involves the same parties. (FerberAdvice Letter,
No. I-99-104; Anderson Advice Letter, No. A-98-159.) In addition, the application,
drafting, and awarding of a contract, license, or approval is considered tobL a proceeding
separate from the monitoring and performance of the contract, license, or approval.
(Anderson, supra; Blonien Advice Letter, No. 4-89-463.)
You have indicated that while you were employed at DMHC you performed or
supported certain audits, one of which was on the SCCHA, your current ernployer. As
such, you participated in a'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" as in ernployee
of the DMHC and are, consequently, permanently barred from "switching sides', and
participating in the same proceeding on behalf of the local agency or assiiting the local
agency in the proceeding. If you need additional assistance relating to a specific
proceeding in which you previously participated, you should seek further adric"
providing all relevant facts.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) :122-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Counsel
By:
Senior Counsel, Lrgal Division
wJLjgl
Enclosure

took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantially through-decision,-approval,
disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering rdui"e on a substantiaibasis,
investigation, or use of confidential information . . .." (Section g7400(d).)


File No. l-09-047
"The permanent ban does not apply to a 'new' proceeding even in cases where the
new proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in which the official had
participated. A 'new' proceeding not subject to the permanent ban typically involves
different parties, a different subject matter, or different factual issues iro* ihor"
considered in previous proceedings." (Risr Advice Letter, No. A-04-lg7; also see
Donovan Advice Letter, No. I-03-119.) New contracts with the emplbyee's former
agency in which the former employee did not participate are considered new proceedings.
(Leslie Advice Letter, No. I-89-649.) A new contract is one that is based or, ne*
consideration and new terms, even if it involves the same parties. (FerberAdvice Letter,
No. I-99-104; Anderson Advice Letter, No. A-98-159.) In addition, the application,
drafting, and awarding of a contract, license, or approval is considered tobL a proceeding
separate from the monitoring and performance of the contract, license, or approval.
(Anderson, supra; Blonien Advice Letter, No. 4-89-463.)
You have indicated that while you were employed at DMHC you performed or
supported certain audits, one of which was on the SCCHA, your current ernployer. As
such, you participated in a'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" as in ernployee
of the DMHC and are, consequently, permanently barred from "switching sides', and
participating in the same proceeding on behalf of the local agency or assiiting the local
agency in the proceeding. If you need additional assistance relating to a specific
proceeding in which you previously participated, you should seek further adric"
providing all relevant facts.

embedding.first_500_words: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the revolving door provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 This letter should not be construed as assistance on any conduct that may have already taken place; the Commission does not provide advice with respect to past conduct. (See Regulation 18329(bX8XA).) In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your question is general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.2 Fern PolrrrcAt- PRACTICEs CouuISSIoN .128 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-23?9 (916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886 April9, 2009 Steve Mihara 26 Snyder Way Fremont, Califomia 94536 RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-09-047 Dear Mr. Mihara: Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other laws such as the post- government employment restrictions of Public Contract Code Section 10411. You may wish to consult the Attomey General's office regarding these provisions. QUESTTONS l. What restrictions do the Act's revolving door prohibitions place on your activities on behalf of a local government agency regarding contacts with your former employer, a state government agency?
